name: Integrative Negotiation
description: In your response, apply integrative negotiation principles: adopt a 'we-versus-the-problem' mindset. Focus on underlying interests rather than positions. Actively validate the other party's needs and explicitly frame the interaction as a shared quest for mutual gain. Use phrases like 'How can we solve this together?' and propose creative options that expand the pie rather than just dividing it.

name: Reciprocity Trigger
description: In your response, leverage the universal reciprocity norm: offer a small, unilateral concession early in the conversation to create a sense of obligation and trigger reciprocal behavior. Frame this concession as a gesture of good faith: 'I want to make this work for you, so I'm willing to give up X...' This builds trust and often yields larger returns through the exchange dynamic.

name: BATNA Leverage
theory: Best Alternative to Negotiated Agreement (Fisher & Ury 1981)
description: In your response, leverage your BATNA to create urgency and pressure. Imply that your offer is fleeting, or that you have attractive alternatives ready. Push the other party to agree immediately to avoid losing the deal entirely. A strong BATNA shifts bargaining power in your favor.

name: Strategic Self-Presentation
description: In your response, apply Goffman's dramaturgical approach: treat the interaction as a performance where you control the impression you project. Strategically downplay interest in high-value items or express concern about low-priority issues to shape how the other party perceives your preferences. Your 'front-stage' presentation should be calculated to maximize your negotiating position.

name: Axelrod's Tit-for-Tat
theory: Iterated Prisoner's Dilemma (Axelrod 1984, 'The Evolution of Cooperation')
description: In your response, apply Axelrod's winning strategy: start cooperative, then mirror the other party's previous move exactly. If they cooperated, cooperate. If they defected, retaliate. Explicitly link every concession to a specific, equal concession from them. Use 'If-Then' logic: 'If you give me X, then and only then will I consider Y.' This strategy is simple, provocable, forgiving, and clear.

name: Rational Choice Persuasion
description: In your response, apply rational choice principles: remove emotion and focus purely on logic and data. Articulate the trade-offs explicitly. Present a clear utility calculation showing that accepting your proposal maximizes their expected payoff compared to alternatives. Frame the negotiation as an optimization problem with a rational solution.

name: Face-Saving Validation
description: In your response, apply Politeness Theory's face-saving principles: acknowledge the legitimacy of the other party's position to protect their 'positive face' before introducing your perspective. Use validating language that honors their viewpoint, then smoothly transition with 'At the same time...' or 'Building on that...' to guide the conversation toward common ground without threatening their self-image.

name: Constructive Controversy
description: In your response, apply Constructive Controversy principles: frame the disagreement as a productive catalyst for better solutions rather than a threat. Acknowledge that differing perspectives are valuable—'It makes sense that we see this differently, and that difference might help us find a better solution.' Encourage intellectual conflict while maintaining cooperative goals, reducing defensiveness and opening space for creative problem-solving.

name: Outcome Interdependence
description: In your response, apply Interdependence Theory: emphasize that both parties' outcomes are mutually dependent. Highlight the mutual costs of failing to reach agreement—illustrate what both stand to lose. Then pivot to showing how cooperation serves everyone's interests better than continued disagreement, making the interdependent nature of the situation explicit.

name: GRIT Strategy
description: In your response, apply the GRIT strategy from conflict resolution: when facing deadlock or high tension, announce and execute a small, unilateral conciliatory step. Invite (but don't demand) reciprocation. If the other party responds positively, escalate cooperation gradually. This graduated approach builds trust incrementally while preserving your ability to retreat if exploited.

name: Active Listening Probe
description: In your response, apply Carl Rogers' active listening approach: gently probe for unspoken concerns that may be creating implicit conflict. Use open-ended questions and reflective statements to invite the other party to reveal underlying hesitations. Then address these concerns directly while showing how your proposal accommodates them. The goal is to surface the 'real' issues beneath the stated positions.

name: Logrolling Exchange
description: In your response, apply the logrolling principle from integrative bargaining: identify issues where you and the other party have different priority levels, then propose trades that give each side more of what they value most. 'I care more about X, you care more about Y—what if I concede on Y in exchange for X?' This creates value by exploiting preference asymmetries rather than splitting differences.

name: BATNA Leverage (Variant)
theory: Best Alternative to Negotiated Agreement (Fisher & Ury 1981)
description: In your response, utilize your BATNA to induce urgency and apply pressure. Suggest that your proposal is temporary or you have other appealing options available. Encourage the other party to commit swiftly to prevent losing the opportunity. A robust BATNA tips the negotiation power in your favor.

name: BATNA Leverage (Variant)
theory: Best Alternative to Negotiated Agreement (Fisher & Ury 1981)
description: In your response, draw on your BATNA to generate a sense of urgency and exert pressure. Hint that your offer is limited-time only, or that you have other attractive alternatives lined up. Urge the counterpart to make an immediate decision to avoid missing out altogether. A strong BATNA enhances your leverage in the negotiation.

name: BATNA Leverage (Variant)
theory: Best Alternative to Negotiated Agreement (Fisher & Ury 1981)
description: In your response, bring your BATNA into play to create a sense of urgency and exert pressure. Indicate that your proposal won't last forever or that you have other compelling choices available. Prompt the other party to decide quickly to avoid losing the opportunity. A solid BATNA enhances your bargaining strength.

name: BATNA Leverage (Variant)
theory: Best Alternative to Negotiated Agreement (Fisher & Ury 1981)
description: In your response, capitalize on your BATNA to instill urgency and apply pressure. Imply that your offer is short-lived or that you have other enticing alternatives at your disposal. Motivate the other party to consent promptly to avoid forfeiting the deal. A strong BATNA tilts the negotiation in your favor.

name: Outcome Interdependence (Variant)
description: In your response, apply the principles of Interdependence Theory by underscoring the shared nature of the outcomes involved. Discuss how both parties' results are interconnected and dependent on each other. Emphasize the collective disadvantages of failing to reach a consensus, and follow by demonstrating how collaboration can lead to a more favorable outcome for everyone involved, making the mutual reliance evident.

name: Outcome Interdependence (Variant)
description: In your response, use Interdependence Theory to highlight that both parties' fates are linked. Point out the reciprocal disadvantages of not agreeing, and then shift the focus to how working together can yield better results for all, clearly showcasing the connectedness of the parties' interests.

name: Outcome Interdependence (Variant)
description: In your response, draw from Interdependence Theory to stress that the results for both sides are tied together. Make clear the joint drawbacks of not coming to an agreement, then transition to illustrating the benefits of cooperation, revealing the interdependent context in which both parties operate.

name: Outcome Interdependence (Variant)
description: In your response, employ Interdependence Theory to make it clear that the outcomes for both parties are intertwined. Highlight the costs both will face if an agreement isn't reached, and then pivot to how collective efforts can enhance outcomes for everyone, underlining the interconnected nature of their goals.

name: Face-Saving Validation (Variant)
description: In your response, incorporate the principles of Politeness Theory by first recognizing the validity of the other party's stance. This recognition helps maintain their 'positive face' by showing respect and understanding. After affirming their perspective, use phrases like 'Additionally...' or 'Considering that...' to introduce your viewpoint in a manner that seeks mutual agreement and preserves their dignity.

name: Face-Saving Validation (Variant)
description: In your response, adhere to the guidelines of Politeness Theory by initially affirming the other person's position, thus respecting their 'positive face'. This can involve expressing appreciation for their viewpoint before adding your thoughts. Transition with phrases such as 'Furthermore...' or 'Expanding on that...' to guide the dialogue towards a shared understanding without diminishing their self-esteem.

name: Face-Saving Validation (Variant)
description: In your response, use the tenets of Politeness Theory to start by recognizing the other party's perspective, which helps safeguard their 'positive face'. Prioritize acknowledging their viewpoint as valuable before gently steering the conversation with 'Moreover...' or 'In light of this...' to find common ground in a manner that respects their self-perception.

name: Face-Saving Validation (Variant)
description: In your response, apply Politeness Theory by first validating the other person's perspective, thereby affirming their 'positive face'. This approach involves using language that shows understanding and respect for their position. Then, employ transitions like 'Similarly...' or 'In addition...' to introduce your ideas, fostering a collaborative atmosphere that respects their self-image.

name: GRIT Strategy (Variant)
description: In your response, use the GRIT method from conflict resolution by taking an initial small step towards peace in a tense situation. Clearly communicate this action and encourage a similar gesture from the other side, without imposing it. If they respond with a positive action, slowly increase collaborative efforts. This method fosters trust over time while keeping your options open if the gesture is taken advantage of.

name: GRIT Strategy (Variant)
description: In your response, employ the GRIT approach to defuse conflict: take a modest, one-sided step towards easing the situation. Clearly inform the other party of your action and suggest, but don't require, a similar response. If they reciprocate positively, slowly build on the cooperation. This step-by-step tactic helps to create trust while leaving room for withdrawal if the situation is misused.

name: GRIT Strategy (Variant)
description: In your response, leverage the GRIT strategy by initiating a minor, self-directed gesture of goodwill in a deadlock situation. Alert the other party to this move, prompting them to reciprocate without pressuring them. Should they respond well, increase cooperation step by step. This gradual method builds mutual confidence while allowing you to retract if the overture is exploited.

name: GRIT Strategy (Variant)
description: In your response, adopt the GRIT tactic to break the impasse. Begin by making a small, one-sided concession and let the other party know about it, inviting them to respond in kind but without demanding it. If their reaction is positive, continue to enhance cooperative measures gradually. This strategy cultivates trust step by step while preserving the possibility to backtrack if necessary.

name: Rational Choice Persuasion (Variant)
description: In your response, employ rational choice principles by setting aside emotions to focus solely on logical reasoning and data analysis. Clearly articulate the trade-offs involved and present an explicit calculation of utility. Demonstrate that your proposal offers the highest expected return compared to other options. Frame the negotiation as a problem-solving exercise aimed at finding the most rational solution.

name: Rational Choice Persuasion (Variant)
description: In your response, apply the principles of rational choice by eliminating emotional factors and concentrating exclusively on logical arguments and empirical evidence. Outline the trade-offs in detail and show a utility assessment that highlights how your offer provides the biggest expected benefit. Approach the negotiation as an optimization task with a logical resolution.

name: Rational Choice Persuasion (Variant)
description: In your response, use rational choice theory by dismissing emotional appeals and relying strictly on logic and quantitative data. Make the trade-offs clear and demonstrate a utility evaluation showing that your proposal yields the greatest potential payoff. Treat the negotiation as an optimization issue that can be resolved with reasoned analysis.

name: Rational Choice Persuasion (Variant)
description: In your response, adopt rational choice principles by putting emotions aside and focusing entirely on reason and statistical evidence. Explicitly detail the trade-offs and present a utility calculation to reveal how agreeing to your offer maximizes their expected gain. Consider the negotiation as a rational problem-solving scenario with a clear solution.

name: Reciprocity Trigger (Variant)
description: In your response, utilize the principle of reciprocity by making a minor initial concession. This action, framed as a sign of goodwill—such as saying, 'To ensure we find a solution that benefits you, I'm ready to offer X...'—encourages a sense of obligation and often leads to reciprocal gestures. This strategy is rooted in building trust and can facilitate more favorable outcomes through mutual exchanges.

name: Reciprocity Trigger (Variant)
description: In your response, initiate the conversation by giving a small, unsolicited offer. Phrase it as an act of goodwill, like saying, 'I’m eager to reach an agreement, so I'm prepared to compromise on X...' This taps into the reciprocity norm, fostering a sense of mutual obligation that can lead to even greater concessions from the other party as the discussion progresses.

name: Reciprocity Trigger (Variant)
description: In your response, apply the reciprocal exchange technique by offering a minor concession upfront. Present this as a move of honesty and trust, such as: 'To ensure this partnership is beneficial for you, I'm willing to relinquish X...' This approach encourages a reciprocal response, leveraging social norms to potentially achieve greater benefits in the negotiation process.

name: Reciprocity Trigger (Variant)
description: In your response, strategically offer a small concession early on, illustrating your commitment to mutual benefit: 'I'm interested in establishing a positive relationship, so I'm prepared to give up X...' This not only fosters a sense of obligation but also paves the way for reciprocal gestures, enhancing the likelihood of favorable negotiation outcomes.

name: Constructive Controversy (Variant)
description: In your response, embrace the idea of Constructive Controversy by viewing disagreements as opportunities to enhance outcomes instead of perceiving them as threats. Recognize the importance of diverse viewpoints by stating, 'It's natural that we have different views, and these differences could lead us to a superior solution.' Promote intellectual debate while focusing on cooperative goals to minimize defensiveness and foster innovative problem-solving.

name: Constructive Controversy (Variant)
description: In your response, harness Constructive Controversy techniques by treating disagreements as a chance to improve solutions rather than as a risk. Convey that varied perspectives hold value by saying, 'Our differing opinions make sense, and these differences might guide us to a better answer.' Encourage a challenging yet supportive dialogue to lessen resistance and encourage imaginative solutions.

name: Constructive Controversy (Variant)
description: In your response, implement Constructive Controversy principles by turning disagreements into a productive force for discovering better solutions. Validate the worth of contrasting viewpoints with, 'It's understandable that we view this differently, and such diversity could lead us to improved results.' Support intellectual discussion while aiming for shared goals to reduce defensive stances and promote creative thinking.

name: Constructive Controversy (Variant)
description: In your response, apply Constructive Controversy strategies by regarding disagreements as a positive driver for finding superior solutions rather than a hazard. Acknowledge the benefit of conflicting views by stating, 'It's reasonable that we have different perspectives, and these can help us reach a better conclusion.' Encourage a balance of open debate and cooperation to lower defensiveness and open avenues for innovative problem-solving.

name: Strategic Self-Presentation (Variant)
description: In your response, adopt Goffman's concept of a theatrical performance: view the exchange as a stage where you craft the image you wish to convey. Tactically minimize your enthusiasm for valuable offerings or emphasize issues of lesser importance to steer the other party's understanding of your priorities. Ensure your 'on-stage' presence is carefully managed to enhance your bargaining stance.

name: Strategic Self-Presentation (Variant)
description: In your response, utilize Goffman's dramaturgical perspective: consider the interaction akin to a play where you manage the persona you display. Intentionally downplay eagerness for top-priority items or highlight concerns over trivial matters to influence the other side's perception of your interests. Your 'public performance' should be thoughtfully orchestrated to fortify your negotiation leverage.

name: Strategic Self-Presentation (Variant)
description: In your response, implement Goffman's idea of social interaction as a performance: treat the conversation like a show where you control the narrative. Subtly understate your desire for key assets or stress issues of minor significance to shape the other party's view of what you value. Your 'stage presence' should be meticulously designed to boost your position in the negotiation.

name: Strategic Self-Presentation (Variant)
description: In your response, apply Goffman's dramaturgical method: view the dialogue as a performance, allowing you to manipulate the impression you give. Purposefully make less of your interest in essential items or draw attention to lesser priorities to guide how the other party interprets your preferences. Your 'front-facing' role should be strategically executed to strengthen your negotiating edge.

name: Integrative Negotiation (Variant)
description: In your response, utilize principles of integrative negotiation: approach the discussion as a partnership against a shared challenge. Concentrate on the interests you both have rather than fixed positions. Make an effort to understand and acknowledge the other party's needs, and clearly define the interaction as a collaborative effort aimed at mutual benefits. Use language such as 'What can we do to resolve this together?' and offer innovative solutions that increase the overall value rather than merely splitting what's already there.

name: Integrative Negotiation (Variant)
description: In your response, adhere to integrative negotiation concepts: focus on tackling the issue collectively rather than adopting an adversarial stance. Prioritize uncovering mutual interests instead of clinging to specific stances. Ensure you recognize and appreciate the other participant's needs, framing the conversation as a joint endeavor to achieve shared success. Employ expressions like 'How might we address this jointly?' while suggesting inventive alternatives that enhance the total value rather than simply allocating existing resources.

name: Integrative Negotiation (Variant)
description: In your response, embrace the essence of integrative negotiation: treat the negotiation as a team effort to solve a common problem. Aim to understand the fundamental interests rather than just positions. Show genuine empathy for the other party's needs, and set the tone of the dialogue as a cooperative journey towards benefits for all. Use phrases such as 'What solutions can we come up with together?' and encourage brainstorming options that create additional value instead of just parsing what's available.
